Jonadab was known for wisdom and devotion. He lived nearly 300 years before. Jonadab instructed his descendants not to drink wine. He also appointed them to live in tents, or movable dwellings: this would teach them not to think of settling anywhere in this world. To remain humble would be the way to continue living long in the land where they were strangers. Humility and contentment are always the best policy, and people's surest protection. Also, that they might not fall into unlawful pleasures, they were to deny themselves even lawful delights. The thought that we are strangers and pilgrims should oblige us to abstain from all fleshly lusts. Let them have little to lose, and then difficult times would be less dreadful: let them hold lightly to what they had, and then they might lose it with less pain. Those are in the best frame to meet suffering who live a life of self-denial, and who despise the vanities of the world. Jonadab's descendants observed these rules strictly, only using proper means for their safety in a time of general suffering.

The trial of the Rechabites’ constancy was for a sign; it made the disobedience of the Jews to God more noticeable. The Rechabites were obedient to one who was but a man like themselves, and Jonadab never did for his descendants what God has done for his people. Mercy is promised to the Rechabites. We are not told about the fulfillment of this promise; but it was doubtless fulfilled, and travelers say the Rechabites may be found as a separate people even today. Let us follow the advice of our pious ancestors, and we shall find good in so doing.
